C[gillessed: there's lots of resources online on how to play
gillessed: okay, in return, when I'm done, will you change your rank?
gillessed: lemme resign so we can go into review mode, will be easier.
gillessed: wait, won't let me do it eyt >.>
Lycus: is this world's oldest board game?
gillessed: the basic idea of the game
gillessed: is to surround empty squares.
gillessed: yeah it's a little awkward to explain with showing >.>
C[gillessed: so if you see, i'm starting to take area in the top left
C[gillessed: and it's looking like i might surround something on the right
gillessed: the one big rule is how to capture stones
gillessed: \\if i surround it on all edges
gillessed: i capture it and take it off the board
gillessed: same works for a group of stones
C[gillessed: here's what i tell people
gillessed: it's not gonna very fun when starting out if you just chose this randomly
gillessed: since there's a lot of strategy and depth
Lycus: don't tell me there are world cup tournaments and such
gillessed: you'll start out by losing to stronger players quickly
Lycus: old men playing with stones
gillessed: chess, albeit though a little less abstract
gillessed: is similar in terms of game class
Lycus: yea but you know what i mean
gillessed: i know how to play
gillessed: and played a bit as a kid
gillessed: but once i started playing go, i never looked back
Lycus: if this game is so popular how come i've never heard of it
Lycus: and i am sure im not the only one
gillessed: so it's very very popular in asia
gillessed: but in the western world
Lycus: do you live in asia
gillessed: it hasn't gotten very far yet
gillessed: but i got introducted to the game through anime
Lycus: can a game take hours to finish?
gillessed: and there was a club at my university
gillessed: top players playing for a lot of money
gillessed: those are played over two days
gillessed: most of them usually last a couple of hours
gillessed: amateurs games tend to be under an hour
gillessed: if two people's rank gap is 5, the weaker player places 5 stones first
gillessed: when you get that strong
gillessed: you can take professional tests (basically a tournament of strong players)
Lycus: so how do you win
Lycus: when you are out of stones?
gillessed: at the end of the game
gillessed: when both players mutually pass
gillessed: i.e. neither player wants to play another stone
gillessed: there will be areas of territory surrounded by both players
gillessed: the ""deciding when to pass"" part
gillessed: is the generally the hardest part about learning the game
gillessed: the end of a chess game is a lot easier to comprehend
gillessed: anyways, i gotta run, if you're interested in playing more, here's a website that goes over the rules quite nicely
gillessed: it hasn't really been updated since like the 90s
gillessed: but it's got a fantastic explanation of the rules
Lycus: can you memorize different patterns like in chess

C[Schlynn: when you guys are filling in space and making eyes and walls and everything (late-mid game), how automated is that?  like is it just lines of play you know will result in at least a tie for example?

C[gillessed: @Schlynn, mmm, it's more like we see a sequence, and we sort of try and follow that as far as we go. It's not ""automated"", it's more like we have a plan, and we're trying to follow it. However, the opponent doesn't always play where you expect them, in which case you'll have to try again, and make a new plan or sequence.
